Q1: I tried to build a consistent routine, honestly I did. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found that I needed to physically do things to understand them, so I'd schedule blocks of time where I could work through problems hands-on. With about 23 hours a week dedicated to studying, I'd spread it across most days, usually in the afternoons after my part-time job. The stress has been pretty high lately, so sometimes my carefully planned schedule would fall apart, but I always tried to get back on track.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I would have liked. I aimed for regularity, maybe studying the same times on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, but life has a way of interfering. When deadlines approached, everything intensified and I'd find myself putting in longer sessions. With limited resources, I sometimes had to prioritize work shifts over study time, which threw off my rhythm. The stress of juggling everything made it hard to maintain the consistency I wanted.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new assignments or looking up lecture materials that prompted me to log in. I'd also log in when I felt that familiar wave of anxiety about whether I'd missed something important. Sometimes after peer support sessions, I'd feel motivated to check in and see what I needed to work on next. The discussion boards occasionally pulled me in too, though I wasn't the most active participant there.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. I'd wake up, make coffee, and open the LMS while eating breakfast. It happened naturally once I started seeing the value in staying on top of announcements and new materials. The routine helped manage my stress levels because I felt more in control of what was coming up.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful for me, and I watched a lot of them, nearly 300 minutes worth. As someone who learns by doing, I appreciated when videos showed practical demonstrations I could follow along with. The assignment feedback was also valuable because it gave me concrete things to work on. I found that resources I could interact with or apply immediately stuck with me much better than passive reading.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't engage much with peer interactions on the platform, showing zero recorded interactions there. It's not that I didn't value collaboration, but I found it easier to connect with people face-to-face through the peer support program. Some of the supplementary readings I skimmed or skipped entirely when time was tight. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about where I invested my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exam preparation. If I could physically work through something and feel myself getting better at it, that was gold. The activities that let me practice and make mistakes without penalty were the ones I gravitated toward. Honestly, anything that reduced my stress by making me feel more prepared was automatically worth it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. With required work, there's this clear expectation and consequence that drives you forward. Optional stuff, even when I knew it would help, often got pushed aside when stress levels spiked or when I had to pick up extra work shifts. I wish I'd done more of them, but when you're stretched thin, you have to make hard choices about where your energy goes.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them on paper because I retain things better when I'm writing and physically manipulating information. I used a lot of scratch paper for working through problems, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere in the system. Sometimes I'd study with peers from the support group using shared notes we'd made together.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd have my printed notes spread across my desk, working through concepts for hours, and none of that would register online. I'd watch a video once, take detailed handwritten notes, then review those notes multiple times over the following days. My best learning often happened when I was pacing around my room, talking through concepts out loud to myself. The LMS would show one video view, but the actual learning was so much deeper than that.
Q11: This was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it or take thorough notes, and then that material became part of my offline study arsenal. With my internet being somewhat unreliable and needing to study during commutes or breaks at work, having offline materials was essential. I probably spent three times as long with downloaded content as I did actually logged into the platform.
Q12: There were definitely darker periods where engagement became really difficult. The sadness I've been dealing with sometimes made it hard to find the energy to care about coursework. When stress peaked around midterms, I felt paralyzed and would just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Financial pressures from having limited resources added another layer of worry that competed for mental space.
Q13: All of those factors played a role at different times. Fatigue was a big one because balancing work and study left me exhausted some weeks. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS rather than confront what I didn't understand. Competing responsibilities, especially financial ones, definitely pulled me away from the platform. The cognitive load of managing everything while feeling stressed was sometimes overwhelming.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos or readings and realize fifteen minutes later that I hadn't absorbed anything because my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I was too tired to really engage but felt guilty not at least trying. There were sessions where I was physically present but emotionally checked out, going through the motions without real learning happening. The sadness I've been experiencing made this worse.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts quite a bit, which is why I only have five recorded. Writing publicly about my thoughts felt vulnerable, especially when I wasn't feeling great emotionally. Some assignments got pushed back because the stress of starting them felt insurmountable in the moment. I knew they were important, but sometimes my brain just couldn't cooperate, and I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense stress spikes, but they also forced me to actually complete things I might have otherwise avoided indefinitely. My LMS activity would concentrate heavily in the days before due dates. I'd log in more frequently, watch videos more urgently, and finally engage with materials I'd been putting off. It wasn't ideal, but the external pressure helped overcome my internal resistance.
Q17: Feedback had a significant impact on my engagement. When I received positive feedback on my assignment work, where I scored quite well, it motivated me to keep pushing despite the stress. Constructive criticism helped me focus my limited energy on areas that actually needed improvement. The peer support program provided informal feedback too, which sometimes mattered more than official comments because it felt more personal and immediate.
Q18: The course structure provided a framework that helped me stay oriented even during difficult periods. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to anchor to when everything else felt chaotic. However, the pacing sometimes felt relentless when I was already struggling. I appreciated when there was flexibility built in, but I also needed that structure to keep from completely falling behind.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier given my learning style and resource limitations. What made it harder was when activities required consistent internet access or real-time participation. The discussion requirements felt awkward for me because I process things kinesthetically, not verbally. I would have engaged more if there had been more hands-on project options that let me demonstrate understanding through doing.
Q20: I think that pattern reflects students who are trying hard but struggling to translate effort into output. Maybe they're confused about requirements, or dealing with personal issues that make completion difficult even when they're putting in time. It could also be perfectionism, spending hours on something but never feeling it's good enough to submit. High activity without submissions might show someone who's engaged but blocked by something the system can't see.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. My exam score and assignment quality were decent despite moderate LMS engagement because so much of my learning happened offline. If you learn effectively through methods the platform doesn't track, your activity looks lower than your actual engagement. Students with strong study skills might need fewer platform interactions to achieve the same understanding as someone who needs more guided support.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and which resources students gravitate toward. They capture the digital footprint of engagement pretty well. What they miss is the quality of attention during those sessions, offline studying, emotional state, and the learning that happens through conversation and physical practice. They can't see the student sitting with printed notes at 2 AM, working through problems by hand.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's showing up mentally even when you're struggling, trying to connect concepts to real applications. For me, engagement is physical too, working through problems, discussing with peers, making mistakes and learning from them. It's an active investment of your limited energy into something you believe matters for your growth.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how the peer support program kept me going during my lowest moments, those conversations that happened outside any tracked system. It wouldn't capture the emotional labor of studying while dealing with sadness and high stress. My offline study hours, the handwritten notes, the concepts I worked through while walking to clear my head, none of that appears. Most importantly, it can't show the determination it took to keep engaging despite limited resources and genuine struggle.
